title: Dextran Specificity in Thrombus Inhibition
abstract:
The technique involves the placement of
a plastic
bag
over the kidney. The bag is inverted
over the
kidney after which the renal artery and the ureter
are clamped.
The latter
is
very important
because
otherwise you will have warm blood going into the
kidney via the ureteral artery. Cooling is then carried
out by placing sterile slush in the suspended plastic
bag and stirring it.
In 25 minutes of bag cooling you
can bring the
temperature of the kidney down to 3 to 4 C (37.4-
39.2 F) and then the bag and ice
are removed from
the field.
This technique will
double
to triple
the
length of time that the kidney
can be ischemie.
In
other words, you can actually clamp the renal artery
ll/S hours clinically, we have found,
as compared to
the 30 minutes which we consider safe under normo¬
thermia.
In
the
animal
you
can lengthen
tolerable
renal
ischemia from 2 hours to 4\/$ hours by this technique.
We have used this technique for stones, allowing
bivalving of the kidney. We have used it for vascular
malformations within the kidney where
we wanted
to open the kidney under avascular conditions. And
most commonly this technique has been used for
re¬
constructive procedures on the renal artery. It is true,
however, that most endarterectomies do not require
this technique
as they
can be performed within 25
minutes.
I would like to say finally that, if you do not have
a
sterile plastic bag
or the
ice available,
and you
encounter a situation where you need renal protection,
if you will just free the kidney up and bring it into
the operative field, it will assume room temperature,
which will
give
you considerable
added
protection
against
ischemia
somewhat in the neighborhood of
the protection afforded by the technique described by
Dr. Najafi and associates.
Roger F. Smith, MD, Detroit : Albert Macksood,
while
a resident working in
our surgical laboratory
under the direction of my senior associate, Emerick
Szilagyi,
did
a
similar
experiment
using
cooled
oxygenated whole blood for the perfusate. These find¬
ings were reported at the Surgical Forum in 1961. He
found that 3 / hours is the critical time of devascu-
larization for the canine kidney, and that perfusion
with cooled oxygenated whole blood, maintaining the
kidney between 13-15 C, afforded
a high degree of
protection for this period of time.
We have had the opportunity of using this tech¬
nique
in
two patients undergoing abdominal aortic
aneurysmectomy where it was necessary to reanasto-
mose a renal artery which was directly involved with
the aneurysm. In both cases the method proved simple
to use and achieved the desired protection for the
kidney during the period of ischemia required to ac¬
complish the aneurysm resection and reestablishment
of renal circulation (for 45 and 64 minutes, respec¬
tively).
Edward J. Beattie, MD, Chicago : As for Dr. Javid's
questions : it is true that often the surgeon does not
have to clamp the kidney for more than 25 minutes,
but some conditions might need more than 25 minutes.
Thirty minutes
seems to be the maximum time you
could interrupt
the
circulation
to
a
normothermic
human kidney.
As to the question of cooling in the renal bed. The
problem there is that collateral vessels could let cold
solution out and make it a little harder to cool the
kidney.
We are aware of Dr. Connolly's use of direct slush
on
the kidney.
There
are problems with
ice
slush
being a surface coolant, and with the uncertainty that
the center of the organ is down to the temperature
required.
I think the technique that Dr. Najafi described could
work on the kidney as well.
Dextran Specificity in
Thrombus Inhibition
ROBERT B. SAWYER, MD, DENVER, AND COL JOHN A. MONCRIEF, MC, USA
IN RECENT years a great deal of attention
has been directed to the properties of dextran
other than those of volume expansion.
Con-
siderable emphasis has been given to dextran in
low flow situations7,9,17,19 and in thrombotic
states.5,13-15
Attention
has
centered
on
the
antisludging effects of low molecular weight
dextran (40,000 molecular weight)1,3,9,10,14,20
and the antithrombotic properties of clinical
dextran (75,000 molecular weight). Work in
our laboratories has been primarily directed to
the evaluation of clinical dextran in conditions
Read before the 72nd Annual Session of the West-
ern
Surgical
Association,
Colorado
Springs,
Colo,
Nov 19-21,
1964.
From Denver (Dr. Sawyer), and USA Surgical
Research Unit, BAMC, Fort Sam Houston, Tex (Col
Moncrief, MC).
Reprint requests to Fort Sam Houston, Tex 78234
(Col Moncrief).

 which predispose to thrombosis and thrombus
propagation.15
We demonstrated that clinical dextran would
significantly inhibit intravascular thrombosis
after vein grafts of arteries which are not more
than 4 mm to external diameter, and an ability
to inhibit the propagation of previously formed
thrombi in isolated canine jugular veins.
We
have also reported on its use in the treatment of
thrombophlebitis.15
Since both clinical dextran and low molecular
weight dextran are categorized by the average
molecular weight of the particles, it is of con¬
siderable interest to evaluate the importance of
fractions having more specific molecular weight
ranges. A more rational analysis of the effects
of dextran can be made if the more active frac¬
tions can be determined. The work of Bryant
et al ° has indicated that clinical dextran is more
valuable in preventing thrombosis than are the
lower molecular weight fractions.
It is the purpose of this study to evaluate the
effects that fractions of dextran with sharply
defined molecular weight ranges have on clot
propagation.
Material and Methods
Immunized, healthy,
adult mongrel dogs
anesthe¬
tized with pentobarbital (Nembutal) served as the test
animals. An electrode system composed of
an 8-volt
dry-cell battery and two 5 X 10 mm gold electrodes
was applied to partially occluded intact canine jugular
veins. A 5 ma current was allowed to flow through
the system for
one hour. At the end of this hour
a
well-formed thrombus was consistently formed at the
positive electrode. The occlusion was removed and the
vessel opened five hours later, revealing propagation

of the thrombus for 25 to 30 mm. These animals served
as controls. After the acute thrombus
was formed,
five other groups of animals were given a single dose
of dextran (1.2 gm/kg) and five hours later the veins
opened and the extent of the thrombus propagation
measured.
Dogs showing
no propagation
were
au-
topsied for pulmonary emboli.
Closely cut dextran fractions with average molecular
weights of 9,400; 26,500; 40,000; 50,500; and 77,500
were compared, utilizing
a double blind technique in
randomly selected mongrel dogs.
Fractionation data indicate that 80% of the 77,500
fraction was within the range of 50,800 to 93,500 ; that
80% of
the 50,500 fraction
fell
within
the
range
33,300 to 68,500; 80% of the 40,000 fraction fell in
the range of 16,700 to 58,000; and the 26,700 fraction
had 80% in the range of 16,200 to 36,400.
Results
Fifty propagation preparations were studied.
The gross data are tabulated in Table 1. The
most striking effect is
seen with the dextran
which has a molecular weight closely approxi¬
mating 77,500. An analysis of variance (Table
2) clearly demonstrates
a highly significant
(P<0.001 ) difference between the effect of the
77,500 fraction and the other four fractions. A
less significant (P<0.01) difference is noted
between the two fractions having 50,500 and
40,000 molecular weights,
as compared with
those having 26,500 and 9,400 average molecu¬
lar weights. There is no significant difference
between the 50,500 and 40,000, or between the
26,500 and the 9,400.
Comment
It is important to determine the range of
molecular weight of dextran most effective in
the inhibition of thrombus formation. Dextran
has certain physical properties which enable it
to inhibit sludging and thrombosis and to im-

It is important to determine the range of
molecular weight of dextran most effective in
the inhibition of thrombus formation. Dextran
has certain physical properties which enable it
to inhibit sludging and thrombosis and to im-
Table 2.—Analysis of Variance Shozvs Fraction A to
Be Significantly More Effective
prove blood flow. It coats the red cells and plate¬
lets, and coats the intima, particularly in areas of
injury.4·14 This results in an increase of the nor¬
mal electronegativity of the red cells and in¬
Because of the increased negativity
there is an increase in the normal repulsive force
between formed elements and between the intima
and formed elements. The negative coating on
the vessel wall also blocks the positive current
The volume-expanding properties of dextran
dilute the blood. The physical changes prevent
red cell aggregation, inhibit thrombosis, and
use of dextran for other than simple
volume replacement has shown that high molecu¬
lar weight causes sludging while low molecular
weight inhibits it.9 Allergic reactions and bleed¬
ing tendencies are more common with the high
molecular weight,11 while the low molecular
weight is of more value in low flow situations
than clinical or high molecular weight dextran.
The clinical product is more effective in in¬
hibiting thrombus formation than is the low
While clinical dextran has an average molecu¬
lar weight of 75,000, 5% to 10% of this produce
is found to have a molecular weight as high as
200,000 or as low as 25.000, giving an "80%
spread" of 175,000 in molecular weight. In this
study, the "80% spread" of the dextrans frac¬
tions were of molecular weights from 20,200 to
42,700. Tn the field of dextran chemistry these
are truly closely cut fractions.
The striking effect obtained in using dextran
with an average molecular weight of 77,500 indi¬
cates that this molecular weight has an almost
specific ability to inhibit thrombus propaga¬
slowed thrombosis, are probably of little benefit
in the true inhibition of propagation. It would,
therefore, appear that dextran used in the treat¬
ment of thrombophlebitis or other thromboem¬
bolic conditions should be within the molecular
weight range of from 50,800 to 93,500.
Clinical dextran is the only presently available
product falling mainly within these limits. Other
commercially available dextrans have varying
portions of their molecular weight spectrum
dextran is concentrated there. It would
reasonable thus to utilize clinical dextran for
use in the therapy of vascular thrombotic dis¬
ease in preference to the presently more glamor¬
ous small molecular weight dextran.
In an effort to further identify the most ef¬
fective molecular weight for thrombophlebitis
therapy, closely cut fractions of dextran were
evaluated for their ability to inhibit thrombus
Intravascular thrombi were formed in isolated
jugular veins of randomly selected mongrel
dogs. Two gold electrodes were placed on the
external surface of the temporarily occluded
vein, and a 5 ma current was allowed to flow
for one hour. At the end of the hour the oc¬
clusion was relieved and dextran  .2 gm/kg)
vein was opened and the length of thrombus
with molecular weights closely approximating
9,400; 26,500; 40.000; 50,500;"and 77,500 were
compared, utilizing a double-blind technique.
There was a striking inhibition of thrombus
propagation in the group receiving dextran with
a molecular weight of about 77,500 (P<0.001 ).
Generic and Trade Names of Drugs
Dextran—Dextran, Expandex, Gentran.

HIRAM H. BELDING, MD, RIVERSIDE, CALIF
pulmonary embolism in recent years have as-
sumed an increasing importance to the surgeon
because of lower mortality rates from other
an actual increase in incidence of
decades.1,11,16,17 Many surgeons fail to recog-
nize these complications as a problem in their
Others believe that thrombophlebitis
and pulmonary embolism is common in north
temperate climates and rare in semiarid areas
such as the southwestern United States.
Most surgeons reserve the use of postopera-
tive anticoagulants or sodium heparin for those
patients who either have a previous history of
who actually develop these complications post-
stress the unreliability of the clinical
ters have stated that thromboembolic disease
now accounts for one of the major causes of
death after surgery ranging from 12% to 42%
of all postoperative deaths : 60% to 80% of all
initial pulmonary emboli are unpredictable with
no previous history and are fatal according to
various authors.1·2·11·18 Therefore, as stated by
Allen,1 watchful waiting for thromboembolic
disease to occur is unwarranted and effective
safe prophylaxis is mandatory. Also, it is not
enough to use prophylactic measures only on
those patients with a previous history of throm¬
Many methods of prevention of postopera¬
tive pulmonary embolism have been attempted
ligations and the use of elastic stockings do not
in the opinion of many investigators 7·18·19 offer

tions. McLauchlin and Paterson 9 with autopsy
studies have explained these unsatisfactory re¬
sults by finding that 73% of thrombi arose post-
